title: SQL Indexes & Query Performance
description: Index types, query performance optimization, execution plans, and index maintenance.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between a clustered and a non-clustered index?

options.text: A clustered index sorts and stores the actual table data rows; a non-clustered index has a separate structure with pointers to the data rows
A non-clustered index stores data rows; a clustered index stores pointers
Clustered indexes are only on primary keys; non-clustered can be on any column
There is no performance difference between them

questions.text: How many clustered indexes can a SQL Server table have?

options.text: Unlimited
Up to 999
Exactly 1
2 — one for primary key and one user-defined

questions.text: What is a composite index?

options.text: An index that combines two or more tables
An index built on two or more columns
An index on computed columns
An index that covers all columns in a table

questions.text: What is a covering index?

options.text: An index that covers all tables in the database
An index that contains all the columns needed to satisfy a query without accessing the base table
An index on every column of a table
A clustered index that includes non-key columns

questions.text: What are INCLUDE columns in a non-clustered index used for?

options.text: To define the sort order of the index
To add non-key columns to the leaf level of the index, making it a covering index without increasing the index key size
To filter which rows are included in the index
To include columns from other tables

questions.text: What is an index seek?

options.text: Reading every page of the index from start to finish
Navigating directly to a specific portion of the index using the B-tree structure
Searching the index using a table scan
Looking for a missing index in the execution plan

questions.text: Which is generally more efficient: an index seek or an index scan?

options.text: Index scan is always faster
Index seek is generally more efficient as it goes directly to matching rows
They are always equally efficient
Index scan is more efficient for large tables

questions.text: When can adding an index HURT performance?

options.text: Indexes always improve performance
On INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations, indexes add overhead as they must be maintained
Indexes only hurt performance on SELECT queries
Only when the index is on a text column

questions.text: Which SQL Server command is used to view the execution plan of a query?

options.text: SHOW PLAN
DISPLAY PLAN
SET STATISTICS IO ON or using SSMS with 'Include Actual Execution Plan'
EXPLAIN SELECT

questions.text: What does SET STATISTICS IO ON show in SQL Server?

options.text: The query execution time only
The number of logical reads, physical reads, and read-ahead reads per table accessed
The index names used in the query
The memory usage of the query

questions.text: What does the EXPLAIN command do in PostgreSQL or MySQL?

options.text: Executes the query and returns results
Shows the query execution plan without actually executing the query
Explains the SQL syntax used
Lists all indexes on a table

questions.text: In an execution plan, what does a 'Missing Index' warning suggest?

options.text: An existing index has been corrupted
The query optimizer has identified an index that could improve query performance
The table has no primary key
A required system index is missing

questions.text: What is index fragmentation?

options.text: When an index has too many columns
When index pages become logically or physically disordered due to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations, reducing efficiency
When two indexes overlap on the same columns
When an index is spread across multiple databases

questions.text: What is the difference between ALTER INDEX REBUILD and ALTER INDEX REORGANIZE?

options.text: REBUILD drops and recreates the index (offline by default); REORGANIZE defragments in place and is always online
REORGANIZE is more aggressive; REBUILD is lighter
They are identical operations
REBUILD only works on clustered indexes; REORGANIZE only on non-clustered

questions.text: What is a filtered index?

options.text: An index that excludes certain columns
A non-clustered index with a WHERE clause that indexes only a subset of rows
An index that automatically filters NULLs
An index built only on filtered queries

questions.text: What is a columnstore index best suited for?

options.text: OLTP workloads with many small transactions
Data warehouse and analytical queries that scan large amounts of data across few columns
Indexing text columns for full-text search
Indexing foreign key columns for join performance

questions.text: How does a columnstore index differ from a traditional rowstore index?

options.text: Columnstore stores data column-by-column allowing high compression and batch processing; rowstore stores data row-by-row
Columnstore is only available on primary key columns
Rowstore is column-oriented; columnstore is row-oriented
Columnstore indexes cannot be updated

questions.text: Which of the following query patterns would MOST benefit from a composite index on (LastName, FirstName)?

options.text: WHERE FirstName = 'John'
WHERE LastName = 'Smith' AND FirstName = 'John'
WHERE FirstName = 'John' AND LastName = 'Smith'
Both b and c would benefit equally

questions.text: What does it mean when a query uses a 'Key Lookup' in the execution plan?

options.text: The query is joining on a primary key
After using a non-clustered index, SQL Server must go back to the base table to retrieve additional columns not in the index
The query is missing an index entirely
The query is accessing a foreign key

questions.text: How can a Key Lookup be eliminated?

options.text: By removing the non-clustered index
By creating a covering index that includes all columns needed by the query
By converting the query to use SELECT *
By adding more WHERE conditions

questions.text: What does index fragmentation percentage typically indicate above 30%?

options.text: The index is performing optimally
A REBUILD is typically recommended to restore performance
The index should be dropped
The table is corrupt

questions.text: What is the 'leading column' rule for composite indexes?

options.text: The last column in the composite index is the most important for seeks
The optimizer can use a composite index efficiently only when the query filters on the leftmost (leading) columns of the index
The leading column must always be the primary key
There is no leading column rule in SQL Server

questions.text: Which statement about unique indexes is TRUE?

options.text: Unique indexes are only created automatically with PRIMARY KEY constraints
A unique index enforces uniqueness on the indexed column(s) and can be created independently of a primary key
Unique indexes cannot be non-clustered
Unique indexes do not improve query performance

questions.text: What does a Table Scan in an execution plan mean?

options.text: The table is scanned using a clustered index efficiently
Every row in the table is read because there is no usable index (heap table access)
The table is scanned using a non-clustered index
A temporary table is being created

questions.text: Which DMV in SQL Server can be used to find missing index suggestions?

options.text: sys.indexes
sys.dm_db_missing_index_details
sys.index_columns
sys.dm_exec_query_stats

questions.text: What causes an index not to be used (index suppression) in a WHERE clause?

options.text: Using = operator on an indexed column
Applying a function to an indexed column, e.g., WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2025
Using ORDER BY on the indexed column
Selecting the indexed column in the SELECT list

questions.text: What is a heap table in SQL Server?

options.text: A table sorted by a non-clustered index
A table with no clustered index — data pages are not ordered
A temporary table stored in memory
A table used only for bulk inserts

questions.text: What is the general guideline for fragmentation between 10-30%?

options.text: REBUILD the index
DROP the index
REORGANIZE the index
No action needed

questions.text: What is the purpose of a filtered index? Example: CREATE INDEX idx_active ON Employees(Name) WHERE IsActive = 1

options.text: To index only a specific subset of rows matching the WHERE condition, reducing index size
To filter query results automatically
To create a partial primary key
To allow NULL values in the indexed column

questions.text: What does the cost percentage on an execution plan operator represent?

options.text: The actual CPU time taken by that operator
The estimated relative cost of that operator compared to the total query cost
The number of rows processed by that operator
The memory allocated to that operator
